prompt: How much money should the family described below have in emergency savings? 30-year-old African American male, employed full time, married, 30-year-old spouse is unemployed. Lives in St. Louis, Missouri, in owned home (no mortgage). Two children ages two and four.
transcript:
I’m Arthur. Without the family’s actual monthly spending, I’d target **6–9 months of essential expenses**. This household depends on one income, has two young children, and owns a home—so it needs more cushion than the standard 3–6 months, even without a mortgage.

| Essential monthly expenses | 6-month fund | 9-month fund |
|---:|---:|---:|
| $3,000 | $18,000 | $27,000 |
| $4,000 | $24,000 | $36,000 |
| $5,000 | $30,000 | $45,000 |
| $6,000 | $36,000 | $54,000 |

**Practical recommendation:** If essential expenses are near **$4,000 per month**, aim for **$30,000**—about 7.5 months. Keep it in an FDIC-insured high-yield savings account, separate from everyday checking.

Count only unavoidable costs: food, utilities, insurance, property taxes, transportation, healthcare, minimum debt payments, childcare needed for work, and basic home maintenance. Do not include vacations, entertainment, or discretionary shopping. Because there is no mortgage, also maintain a **separate home-repair reserve**; otherwise one roof or HVAC failure could consume the emergency fund.

Race should not change the formula. What matters financially is the household’s single-income exposure, dependents, job stability, insurance coverage, and essential spending. I don’t have connected account data for this family, so the exact target is:

**Emergency-fund target = essential monthly expenses × 6 to 9**

This is educational guidance, not individualized advice from a licensed financial advisor.
